For it is a mad scheme this, that you meditate, my excellent friend—of teaching things that you do not know, since you have taken no care to learn them.

greek_head: Οἶμαι μέν ὦ Σώκρατες ὀλιγάκις Ἀθηναίους βουλεύεσθαι καὶ τοὺς ἄλλους Ἕλληνας πότερα δικαιότερα
greek_tokens: oimh men eimi swkraths oligakis aqhnaios bouleuw kai o allos ellhn poteros dikaios h adikos o men gar toioutos hgeomai dhlos eimi eaw oun peri autos skopaw opoteros sumferw prassw ou gar tautos oimh eimi o te dikaios kai o sumferw alla polus dh lusitelew adikew megas adikhma kai eteros ge oimh dikaios ergazomai ou sumferw tis oun ei ostis malista eteros men o dikaios
english_head: I think, Socrates, that the Athenians and the rest of the Greeks rarely deliberate as to which is the more just or unjust course: for they regard questions of this sort as obvious; and so they pass them over and consider which course will prove more expedient in the result. For the just and the expedient, I take it, are not the same, but many people have profited by great wrongs that they have committed, whilst others, I imagine, have had no advantage from doing what was right. What then? Granti

english_head: are in fact as different as they can be, you surely do not still suppose you know what is expedient for mankind, and why it is so? Well, what is the obstacle, Socrates,—unless you are going to ask me again from whom I learnt it, or how I discovered it for myself? What a way of going on! If your answer is incorrect, and a previous argument can be used to prove it so, you claim to be told something new, and a different line of proof, as though the previous one were like a poor worn-out coat which

english_head: But I shall ignore your sallies in debate, and shall none the less ask you once more, where you learnt your knowledge of what is expedient, and who is your teacher, asking in one question all the things I asked before; and now you will clearly find yourself in the same plight, and will be unable to prove that you know the expedient either through discovery or through learning. But as you are dainty, and would dislike a repeated taste of the same argument, I pass over this question of whether you

greek_head: πότερον δὲ ταὐτά ἐστι δίκαιά τε καὶ συμφέροντ' ἢ ἕτερα τί οὐκ ἀπέδειξας εἰ μὲν βούλει ἐρωτῶν με ὥσπερ
greek_tokens: poteros de tautos eimi dikaios te kai sumferw h eteros tis ou apodeiknumi ei men boulomai erwtaw egw wsper egw sos ei de kai autos epi sautou logos diecerxomai alla ou oida ei oios su an eimi eimi swkraths pros sos dierxomai alla agaqos egw ekklhsia nomizw kai dhmos kai ekei o su dehsis enos ekastos peiqw eimi gar nai oukoun o autos enos te oios te eimi kata monos
english_head: what is expedient for the Athenians: but why have you not made it clear whether the just and the expedient are the same or different? If you like, question me as I did you, or if you prefer, argue out the matter in your own way. But I am not sure I should be able, Socrates, to set it forth to you. Well, my good sir, imagine I am the people in Assembly; even there, you know, you will have to persuade each man singly, will you not? Yes. And the same man may well persuade one person singly,

english_head: and many together, about things that he knows, just as the schoolmaster, I suppose, persuades either one or many about letters? Yes. And again, will not the same man persuade either one or many about number? Yes. And this will be the man who knows—the arithmetician? Quite so. And you too can persuade a single man about things of which you can persuade many? Presumably. And these are clearly things that you know. Yes. And the only difference between the orator

You are insolent, Socrates! This time, at any rate, I am going to have the insolence to persuade you of the opposite of

english_head: No, you yourself must be the speaker. What? Do you not wish above all things to be persuaded? By all means, to be sure. And you would best be persuaded if you should say the case is so? I agree. Then answer; and if you do not hear your own self say that the just is expedient, put no trust in the words of anyone again. I will not: but I may as well answer; for I do not think I shall come to any harm.

greek_head: Μαντικὸς γὰρ εἶ καί μοι λέγε τῶν δικαίων φῂς ἔνια μὲν συμφέρειν ἔνια δ' οὔ
greek_tokens: mantikos gar ei kai egw legos o dikaios fhmi enioi men sumferw enioi de ou nai tis de o men kalos autos eimi o de ou pws outos erwtaw ei tis hdh su dokew aisxros men dikaios de prassw ou egw alla pas o dikaios kai kalos nai tis de au o kalos poteros pas agaqos h o men o de ou oiomai egw eimi swkraths enioi o kalh kakos eimi eimi kai aisxros agaqos nai
english_head: You are quite a prophet! Now tell me, do you consider some just things to be expedient, and others not? Yes. And again, some noble, and some not? What do you mean by that question? I would ask whether anyone ever seemed to you to be doing what was base and yet just. Never. Well, are all just things noble? Yes. And what of noble things, in their turn? Are they all good, or some only, while others are not? In my opinion, Socrates, some noble things are evil. And some base things are good? Yes.

greek_head: Ἆρα λέγεις τὰ τοιάδε οἷον πολλοὶ ἐν πολέμῳ βοηθήσαντες ἑταίρῳ ἢ οἰκείῳ τραύματα ἔλαβον καὶ ἀπέθανον

english_head: Do you mean as in one of the many cases where men have gone to rescue a comrade or kinsman in battle, and have been either wounded or killed, while those who did not go to the rescue, as duty bade, have got off safe and sound? Precisely. And such a rescue you call noble, in respect of the endeavor to save those whom it was one’s duty to save; and this is courage, is it not? Yes. But you call it evil, in respect of the deaths and wounds? Yes.

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν οὐκ ἄλλο μὲν ἡ ἀνδρεία ἄλλο δὲ ὁ θάνατος Πάνυ γε
greek_tokens: ara oun ou allos men o andreia allos de o qanatos panu ge ou ara kata tautos ge eimi kalos kai kakos o o filos bohqew ou fainw oraw toinun ei h ge kalos kai agaqos wsper kai entauqa kata o andreia gar omologew kalos eimi o bohqeia outos oun autos skopaw o andreia agaqos h kakos wde de skopaw su poteros an dexomai su eimi agaqos h kakos agaqos
english_head: And is not the courage one thing, and the death another? Certainly. Then it is not in the same respect that rescuing one’s friends is noble and evil? Apparently not. Then see if, inasmuch as it is noble, it is also good; for in the present case you were admitting that the rescue was noble in respect of its courage: now consider this very thing, courage, and say whether it is good or bad. Consider it in this way: which would you choose to have, good things or evil? Good.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν τὰ μέγιστα μάλιστα Ναί
greek_tokens: oukoun o megas malista nai kai hkistos o toioutos dexomai an steromai pws gar ou pws oun legw peri andreia epi posos an autos dexomai steromai oude zaw an egw dexomai deilos eimi esxatos ara kakh eimi su dokew o deilia egw ek isos o qnhskw ws eoika fhmi oukoun qanatos te kai deilia enantios zwh kai andreia nai
english_head: And most of all, the greatest goods, and of such things you would least allow yourself to be deprived? To be sure. Then what do you say of courage? At what price would you allow yourself to be deprived of it? I would give up life itself if I had to be a coward. Then you regard cowardice as the uttermost evil. I do. On a par with death, it seems. Yes. And life and courage are the extreme opposites of death and cowardice? Yes.

greek_head: Καὶ τὰ μὲν μάλιστ' ἂν εἶναι βούλοιό σοι τὰ δὲ ἥκιστα
greek_tokens: kai o men malista an eimi boulomai su o de hkistos nai ara ostis o men ariston hgeomai o de kakos panu ge en o ariston ara su hgeomai andreia eimi en o kakos qanatos egw o ara bohqew en polemos o filos h men kalos kata agaqos pracis o o andreia kalos autos proseipon fainw ge kata de ge kakos pracis o o qanatos kakos nai oukoun wde dikaios prosagoreuw ekastos o pracis eiper h kakos apergazomai kakos kalew kai h
english_head: And you would most desire to have the former, and least the latter? Yes. Is that because you think the former best, and the latter worst? To be sure. So you reckon courage among the best things, and death among the worst. I do. Then the rescue of one’s friends in battle, inasmuch as it is noble in respect of the working of good by courage, you have termed noble? Apparently. But evil, in respect of the working of evil by death? Yes. So we may fairly describe each of these workings as follows: as

greek_head: ἀγαθὸν ἀγαθὴν κλητέον Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ
greek_tokens: agaqos agaqos klhteos egw dokew ara oun kai h agaqos kalos h de kakos aisxros nai o ara en o polemos o filos bohqeia legos kalos men eimi kakos de oudeis diaferontws legw h ei proseipon autos agaqos men kakos de alhqhs egw dokew legw eimi swkraths oudeis ara o kalh kaqa osos kalos kakos oude o aisxros kaqa osos aisxros agaqos
english_head: so you must call it good because of the good it produces. I believe that is so. And again, are they noble inasmuch as they are good, and base inasmuch as they are evil? Yes. Then in saying that the rescue of one’s friends in battle is noble and yet evil, you mean just the same as if you called the rescue good, but evil. I believe what you say is true, Socrates. So nothing noble, in so far as it is noble, is evil, and nothing base, in so far as it is base, is good.

greek_head: Οὐ φαίνεται Ἔτι τοίνυν καὶ ὧδε σκέψαι ὅστις καλῶς πράττει
greek_tokens: ou fainw eti toinun kai wde skeptomai ostis kalos prassw ou kai eu prassw nai e de eu prassw ou eudaimwn pws gar ou oukoun eudaimwn dia agaqos ktaomai malista ktaomai de outos o eu kai kalos prassw nai o eu ara prassw agaqos pws de ou oukoun kalos o eupragia nai
english_head: Apparently. Now then, consider it again in this way: whoever does nobly, does well too, does he not? Yes. And are not those who do well happy? Of course. And they are happy because of the acquisition of good things? Certainly. And they acquire these by doing well and nobly? Yes. So doing well is good? Of course. And welfare is noble? Yes.

greek_head: Ταὐτὸν ἄρα ἐφάνη ἡμῖν πάλιν αὖ καλόν τε καὶ ἀγαθόν
greek_tokens: tautos ara fainw egw palin au kalos te kai agaqos fainw ostis an ara euriskw kalos kai agaqos euriskw ek ge outos o logos anagkh tis de o agaqos sumferw h ou sumferw mnhmoneuw oun peri o dikaios pws omologew oimh ge o o dikaios prassw anagkaion eimi kalos prassw oukoun kai o o kalos agaqos nai
english_head: Hence we have seen again that noble and good are the same thing. Apparently. Then whatever we find to be noble we shall find also to be good, by this argument at least. We must. Well then, are good things expedient or not? Expedient. And do you remember what our admissions were about just things? I think we said that those who do just things must do noble things. And that those who do noble things must do good things? Yes.

greek_head: Τὰ δὲ ἀγαθὰ συμφέρειν Ναί
greek_tokens: o de agaqos sumferw nai o dikaios ara eimi alkibiadhs sumferw eimi eoika tis oun outos ou su o legos egw de o erwtaw fainw ws eoika ei oun tis anisthmi sumbouleuw eite aqhnaia eite peparhqiois oiomai gignwskw o dikaios kai o adikos fhmi de eimi o dikaios kakos eniote allos tis h katagelaw an autos epei tugxanw kai su legos
english_head: And that good things are expedient? Yes. Hence just things, Alcibiades, are expedient. So it seems. Well now, are not you the speaker of all this, and I the questioner? I seem to be, apparently. So if anyone stands up to advise either the Athenians or the Peparethians, imagining that he understands what is just and unjust, and says that just things are sometimes evil, could you do other than laugh him to scorn, since you actually say yourself that

greek_head: ὅτι ταὐτά ἐστι δίκαιά τε καὶ συμφέροντα Ἀλλὰ μὰ τοὺς θεούς ὦ Σώκρατες οὐκ οἶδ' ἔγωγε
greek_tokens: ostis tautos eimi dikaios te kai sumferw alla ma o qeos eimi swkraths ou oida egw oude ostis legos alla atexnos eoika atopos exw tote men gar egw eteros dokew sew erwtaw tote de allos eita outos eimi filos agnoew o paqhma tis eimi panu ge oiomai an oun ei tis erwtaw su duo ofqalmos h trew exis kai duo xeir h tessares h allos tis o toioutos tote men eteros an apokrinw tote de allos h aei o autos
english_head: just and expedient are the same? But by Heaven, Socrates, I do not even know what I am saying, I feel altogether in such a strange state! For from moment to moment I change my view under your questioning. And are you unaware, my friend, what this feeling is? I am, quite. Well, do you suppose that if someone should ask you whether you have two eyes or three, two hands or four, or anything else of that sort, you would answer differently from moment to moment, or always the same thing?

greek_head: Δέδοικα μὲν ἔγωγε ἤδη περὶ ἐμαυτοῦ οἶμαι μέντοι τὰ αὐτά

english_head: I begin to have misgivings about myself, but still I think I should make the same answer. And the reason would be, because you know? I think so. Then if you involuntarily give contradictory answers, clearly it must be about things of which you are ignorant. Very likely. And you say you are bewildered in answering about just and unjust, noble and base, evil and good, expedient and inexpedient? Now, is it not obvious that your bewilderment is caused by your ignorance of these things?

greek_head: Ἔμοιγε Ἆρ' οὖν οὕτω καὶ ἔχει ἐπειδάν τίς τι μὴ εἰδῇ
greek_tokens: egw ara oun outws kai exis epeidan tis tis mh oida anagkaion peri outos planaw o yuxh pws gar ou tis oun oida ostis tropos anaptw eis o ouranos ma diw ou egw eimi kai planaw su o doca peri outos ou dhta o de aitios oida h egw frazw frazw ostis eimi filos ou oiomai autos efisthmi ou efisthmi
english_head: I agree. Then is it the case that when a man does not know a thing he must needs be bewildered in spirit regarding that thing? Yes, of course. Well now, do you know in what way you can ascend to heaven? On my word, not I. Is that too a kind of question about which your judgement is bewildered? No, indeed. Do you know the reason, or shall I state it? State it. It is, my friend, that while not knowing the matter you do not suppose that you know it.

greek_head: Πῶς αὖ τοῦτο λέγεις Ὅρα καὶ σὺ κοινῇ ἃ μὴ ἐπίστασαι γιγνώσκεις δὲ
greek_tokens: pws au outos legw oraw kai su koinh o mh efisthmi gignwskw de ostis ou efisthmi planaw peri o toioutos wsper peri oyon skeuasia oida dhpou ostis ou oida panu ge poteros oun autos peri outos docazw opws xrh skeuazw kai planaw h o efisthmi epitrepw outws tis de ei en naias pleos ara docazw an poteros
english_head: Here again, how do you mean? Do your share, in seeing for yourself. Are you bewildered about the kind of thing that you do not know and are aware of not knowing? For instance, you know, I suppose, that you do not know about the preparation of a tasty dish? Quite so. Then do you think for yourself how you are to prepare it, and get bewildered, or do you entrust it to the person who knows? I do the latter. And what if you should be on a ship at sea? Would you think

greek_head: χρὴ τὸν οἴακα εἴσω ἄγειν ἢ ἔξω καὶ ἅτε οὐκ εἰδὼς πλανῷο ἄν ἢ τῷ κυβερνήτῃ ἐπιτρέψας ἂν ἡσυχίαν ἄγοις

english_head: whether the tiller should be moved inwards or outwards, and in your ignorance bewilder yourself, or would you entrust it to the helmsman, and be quiet? I would leave it to him. So you are not bewildered about what you do not know, so long as you know that you do not know? It seems I am not, Then do you note that mistakes in action also are due to this ignorance of thinking one knows when one does not? Here again, how do you mean? We set about acting, I suppose, when we think we know what we are

english_head: Yes. But when people think they do not know, I suppose they hand it over to others? To be sure. And so that kind of ignorant person makes no mistakes in life, because they entrust such matters to others? Yes. Who then are those who make mistakes? For, I take it, they cannot be those who know. No, indeed. But since it is neither those who know, nor those of the ignorant

greek_head: εἰδότες ὅτι οὐκ ἴσασιν ἦ ἄλλοι λείπονται ἢ οἱ μὴ εἰδότες οἰόμενοι δ' εἰδέναι

english_head: who know that they do not know, the only people left, I think, are those who do not know, but think that they do? Yes, only those. Then this ignorance is a cause of evils, and is the discreditable sort of stupidity? Yes. And when it is about the greatest matters, it is most injurious and base? By far. Well then, can you mention any greater things than the just, the noble, the good, and the expedient? No, indeed. And it is about these, you say, that you are bewildered? Yes. But if you are bewilde

greek_head: οὐ μόνον ἀγνοεῖς τὰ μέγιστα ἀλλὰ καὶ οὐκ εἰδὼς οἴει αὐτὰ εἰδέναι
greek_tokens: ou monos agnoew o megas alla kai ou eidoi oiomai autos oida kinduneuw babai ara eimi alkibiadhs oios paqos pasxw o egw onomazw men oknew omws de epei monos eimi rhteos amaqia gar sunoikew eimi beltistos o esxatos ws o logos su kathgorew kai su sautou dio kai aissw ara pros o politikos prin paideuw pasxw de outos ou su monos alla kai e polus o prassw o ode
english_head: that you are not only ignorant of the greatest things, but while not knowing them you think that you do? I am afraid so. Alack then, Alcibiades, for the plight you are in! I shrink indeed from giving it a name, but still, as we are alone, let me speak out. You are wedded to stupidity, my fine friend, of the vilest kind; you are impeached of this by your own words, out of your own mouth; and this, it seems, is why you dash into politics before you have been educated. And you are not alone in this

greek_head: τῆς πόλεως πλὴν ὀλίγων γε καὶ ἴσως τοῦ σοῦ ἐπιτρόπου Περικλέους
greek_tokens: o polis plhn oligos ge kai isos o sew epitropos periklehs legw ge su eimi swkraths ou apo o automatos sofos gignomai alla polus kai sofos suggignomai kai puqokleidhs kai anacagorhs kai nun eti thlikoutos eimi damwn suneimi autos outos eneka tis oun hdh tis eidon sofos otioun adunatew poiew allos sofos o autos wsper os su didaskw gramma autos su eimi sofos kai sos poiew o te allos ostis boulomai eimi gar nai

Yes, you know, Socrates, they say he did not get his wisdom independently, but consorted with many wise men, such as Pythocleides and Anaxagoras ; and now, old as he is, he still confers with Damon for that very purpose. greek_tokens: kaluptra kai allos polus topos kalos kai agaqos eis o kosmos ecairew o o gunh kai onoma exw ekastos o topos apo ekastos o kosmos wste oimh egw ei tis eipon o basileus mhthr cerchs de gunh amhstridi ostis en egw exis sew o uios antitassw o deinomaxh us h eimi kosmos isos acios mna penthkonta ei panu polus o de uios autos gaia pleqron erxiasin oude triakosioi qaumazw an ostis pote pisteuw
english_head: and many other fine and fertile regions reserved for the adornment of the consort; and each of these regions was named after some part of her apparel. So I imagine, if someone should say to the king’s mother Amestris, who was wife of Xerxes, The son of Deinomache intends to challenge your son; the mother’s dresses are worth perhaps fifty minae at the outside, while the son has under three hundred acres at Erchiae, she would wonder to what on earth this

greek_head: ἐν νῷ ἔχει οὗτος ὁ Ἀλκιβιάδης τῷ Ἀρτοξέρξῃ διαγωνίζεσθαι καὶ οἶμαι ἂν αὐτὴν εἰπεῖν ὅτι οὐκ ἔσθ' ὅτῳ ἄλλῳ
greek_tokens: en egw exis outos o alkibiadhs o artocerch diagwnizomai kai oimh an autos eipon ostis ou esqw ostis allos pisteuw outos anhr epixeirew plhn epimeleia te kai sofia outos gar monos acios logos en ellhn epei ei ge puqw ostis alkibiadhs outos nun epixeirew proteros men eths oudepw gignomai sfodra eikosi epeita pantapasi apaideutos pros de outos o erasths autos legw ostis xrh proteros manqanw kai epimeleomai eautou
english_head: Alcibiades could be trusting, that he proposed to contend against Artaxerxes; and I expect she would remark—The only possible things that the man can be trusting to for his enterprise are industry and wisdom; for these are the only things of any account among the Greeks. Whereas if she were informed that this Alcibiades who is actually making such an attempt is, in the first place, as yet barely twenty years old, and secondly, altogether uneducated; and further, that when his lover tells him tha

greek_head: καὶ ἀσκήσαντα οὕτως ἰέναι διαγωνιούμενον βασιλεῖ οὐκ ἐθέλει ἀλλά φησιν ἐξαρκεῖν καὶ ὡς ἔχει οἶμαι ἂν αὐτὴν
greek_tokens: kai askew outws eimi diagwnizomai basileus ou eqelw alla fhmi ecarkew kai ws exis oimh an autos qaumazw te kai eromai tis oun pote eimi ostis pisteuw o meirakion ei oun legw ostis kallos te kai megeqos kai genos kai ploutos kai fusis o yuxazw hgeomai an egw eimi alkibiadhs mainomai pros o para sfeis apoblepw pas o toioutos oimh de kan lampidw o
english_head: before he enters on a contest with the king, he refuses, and says he will do very well as he is; I expect she would ask in surprise, On what, then, can the youngster rely? And if we told her, On beauty, stature, birth, wealth, and mental gifts, she would conclude we were mad, Alcibiades, when she compared the advantages of her own people in all these respects. And I imagine that even Lampido, daughter of Leotychides

greek_head: Λεωτυχίδου μὲν θυγατέρα Ἀρχιδάμου δὲ γυναῖκα Ἄγιδος δὲ μητέρα οἳ πάντες βασιλῆς γεγόνασιν θαυμάσαι ἂν καὶ
greek_tokens: lewtuxidhs men qugathr arxidamos de gunh agis de mhthr e pas basileus gignomai qaumazw an kai outos eis o para sfeis uparxw apoblepw ei su en egw exis o uios autos diagwnizomai outws kakos agw kaitoi ou aisxros dokew eimi ei o o polemios gunh beltiwn peri egw dianoeomai oios xrh eimi sfeis epixeirew h egw peri egw autos alla eimi makarios peiqw egw te kai o en delfoi gramma gignwskw
english_head: and wife of Archidamus and mother of Agis, who have all been kings, would wonder in the same way, when she compared her people’s resources, at your intention of having a contest with her son despite your bad upbringing. And yet, does it not strike you as disgraceful that our enemies’ wives should have a better idea of the qualities that we need for an attempt against them than we have ourselves? Ah, my remarkable friend, listen to me and the Delphic motto,

greek_head: σαυτόν ὅτι οὗτοι ἡμῖν εἰσιν ἀντίπαλοι ἀλλ' οὐχ οὓς σὺ οἴει ὧν ἄλλῳ μὲν οὐδ' ἂν ἑνὶ περιγενοίμεθα εἰ μή περ
greek_tokens: sautou ostis outos egw eimi antipalos alla ou os su oiomai os allos men oude an eis perigignomai ei mh per epimeleia ge an kai texnh os su ei apoleipw kai o onomastos gignomai apoleipw en ellhn te kai barbaros e egw dokew era ws oudeis allos allos tis oun xrh o epimeleia eimi swkraths poiew exis echgeomai pas gar mallon eoika alhqhs erw nai alla gar koinos boulh ostis tropos an ostis
english_head: Know thyself; for these people are our competitors, not those whom you think; and there is nothing that will give us ascendancy over them save only pains and skill. If you are found wanting in these, you will be found wanting also in achievement of renown among Greeks and barbarians both; and of this I observe you to be more enamored than anyone else ever was of anything. Well then, what are the pains that I must take, Socrates? Can you enlighten me? For I must say your words are remarkably like

greek_head: βέλτιστοι γενοίμεθα ἐγὼ γάρ τοι οὐ περὶ μὲν σοῦ λέγω ὡς χρὴ παιδευθῆναι περὶ ἐμοῦ δὲ οὔ οὐ γὰρ ἔσθ' ὅτῳ σου
greek_tokens: beltistos gignomai egw gar su ou peri men sew legos ws xrh paideuw peri egw de ou ou gar esqw ostis su diaferw plhn ge eis tis o epitropos o emos beltiwn eimi kai sofos h periklaw o sos tis outos eimi swkraths qeos eimi alkibiadhs os sos egw ou eaw pro ode o hmeros dialegw os kai pisteuw legos ostis o epifaneia dia oudeis allos su eimi h dia egw
english_head: we can improve ourselves to the utmost. For observe that when I speak of the need of being educated I am not referring only to you, apart from myself; since my case is identical with yours except in one point. What is that ? My guardian is better and wiser than your one, Pericles. Who is he, Socrates? God, Alcibiades, who until this day would not let me converse with you; and trusting in him I say that through no other man but me will you attain to eminence.

greek_head: Παίζεις ὦ Σώκρατες Ἴσως λέγω μέντοι ἀληθῆ ὅτι ἐπιμελείας δεόμεθα
greek_tokens: paizw eimi swkraths isos legos mentoi alhqhs ostis epimeleia deomai mallon men pas anqrwpos atar egw ge kai mala sfodra ostis men egw ou yeudw oude meis ostis ge egw tis oun an poiew ou aporrhteon oude malqakisteon eimi etairos outoi dh prepw ge eimi swkraths ou gar alla skepteos koinh kai egw legos
english_head: You are jesting, Socrates. Perhaps; I am right, however, in saying that we need to take pains—all men rather badly, but we two very badly indeed. As to me, you are not wrong. Nor, I fear, as to myself either. Then what can we do? There must be no crying off or skulking, my good friend. No, for that would indeed be unseemly, Socrates. It would; so let us consider in common. Now tell me:

greek_head: φαμὲν γὰρ δὴ ὡς ἄριστοι βούλεσθαι γενέσθαι ἦ γάρ Ναί
greek_tokens: fhmi gar dh ws aristos boulomai gignomai eimi gar nai tis areth dhlos ostis os e anhr e agaqos e tis agaqos dhlos ostis e prassw o pragma poios ara o ippikos ou dhta para o ippikos gar an eimi nai alla o nautikon legw ou para o nautikos gar an eimi nai alla poios o tinw prassw o aqhnaia e kalos agaqos
english_head: we say, do we not, that we wish to be as good as possible? Yes. In what excellence? Clearly that which is the aim of good men. Good in what? Clearly, good in the management of affairs. What sort of affairs? Horsemanship? No, no. Because we should apply to horsemen? Yes. Well, seamanship, do you mean? No. Because we should apply to seamen? Yes. Well, what sort of thing? The business of what men? Of Athenian gentlemen.

greek_head: Καλοὺς δὲ κἀγαθοὺς λέγεις τοὺς φρονίμους ἢ τοὺς ἄφρονας
greek_tokens: kalos de agaqos legw o fronimos h o afrwn o fronimos oukoun o ekastos fronimos outos agaqos nai o de afrwn ponhros pws gar ou ara oun o skutotomos fronimos eis upodhma ergasia panu ge agaqos ara eis autos agaqos tis de eis imation ergasia ou afrwn o skutotomos nai
english_head: Do you mean by gentlemen the intelligent or the unintelligent? The intelligent. And everyone is good in that wherein he is intelligent? Yes. And bad wherein he is unintelligent? Of course. Then is the shoemaker intelligent in the making of foot-gear? Certainly. So he is good in that article? Good. Well now, is not the shoemaker unintelligent in the making of clothes? Yes.

greek_head: Κακὸς ἄρα εἰς τοῦτο Ναί
greek_tokens: kakos ara eis outos nai o autos ara outos ge o logos kakos te kai agaqos fainw eimi oun legw o agaqos anhr eimi kai kakos ou dhta alla tis pote o agaqos legw o dunamai egw arxw en o polis ou dhpou ippos ge ou dhta alla anqrwpos nai ara kamnw ou alla plew ou fhmi alla qerizw ou
english_head: So he is bad in that? Yes. Then, on this showing, the same man is both bad and good. Apparently. Well, can you say that good men are also bad? No, indeed. But whoever do you mean by the good? I mean those who are able to rule in the city. Not, I presume, over horses? No, no. But over men? Yes. When they are sick? No. Or at sea? I say, no. Or harvesting? No.

greek_head: Ἀλλ' οὐδὲν ποιούντων ἤ τι ποιούντων Ποιούντων λέγω
greek_tokens: alla oudeis poiew h tis poiew poiew legos tis peiraw kai egw dhlow oukoun o kai sumballw eautou kai xraomai allhlwn wsper egw zaw en o polis oukoun anqrwpos legw arxw anqrwpos xraomai nai ara keleusths xraomai ereths ou dhta kubernhtikos gar outos ge areth nai alla anqrwpos legw arxw aulhths anqrwpos
english_head: Doing nothing, or doing something? Doing something, I say. Doing what? Try and let me know. Well, men who do business with each other and make use of one another, as is our way of life in our cities. Then you speak of ruling over men who make use of men? Yes. Over boatswains who make use of rowers? No, no. Because that is the pilot’s distinction? Yes. Well, do you mean ruling over men who are flute-players,

greek_head: ἡγουμένων ᾠδῆς καὶ χρωμένων χορευταῖς Οὐ δῆτα
greek_tokens: hgeomai aoidh kai xraomai xoreuths ou dhta xorodidaskalikos gar outos ge au panu ge alla tis pote legw xraomai anqrwpos anqrwpos oios su eimi arxw koinwnew egw legos politeia kai sumballw pros allhlwn outos arxw o en o polis tis oun outos o texnh wsper an ei su eromai palin o nundi koinwnew nautilia efisthmi arxw tis poiew texnh kubernhtikos
english_head: and who lead the singing and make use of dancers? No, no. Because, again, that is the chorus-teacher’s function? To be sure. But whatever do you mean by being able to rule over men who make use of men? I mean ruling over men in the city who share in it as fellow-citizens, and do business with each other. Well, what art is this? Suppose I should ask you over again, as I did just now, what art makes men know how to rule over fellow-sailors? The pilot’s.

greek_head: Κοινωνούντων δ' ᾠδῆς ὡς νυνδὴ ἐλέγετο τις ἐπιστήμη ποιεῖ ἄρχειν
greek_tokens: koinwnew de aoidh ws nundi legw tis episthmh poiew arxw os su arti legw o xorodidaskalia tis de politeia koinwnew tis kalew episthmh euboulia egw eimi swkraths tis de mhs aboulia dokew eimi o o kubernhths ou dhta alla euboulia
english_head: And what knowledge—to repeat what was said a moment ago—makes them rule over their fellow-singers? That which you just mentioned, the chorus-teacher’s. Well now, what do you call the knowledge of one’s fellow-citizens? Good counsel, I should say, Socrates. Well, and is the pilot’s knowledge evil counsel? No, no. Rather good counsel?

greek_head: Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ εἴς γε τὸ σῴζεσθαι πλέοντας Καλῶς λέγεις τί δέ ἣν σὺ λέγεις εὐβουλίαν
greek_tokens: egw dokew eis ge o swzw plew kalos legw tis de os su legw euboulia eis tis eimi eis o ameinwn o polis dioikew kai swzw ameinwn de dioikew kai swzw tis paragignomai h apogignomai wsper an ei su egw eromai ameinwn dioikew swma kai swzw tis paragignomai h apogignomai eipon an ostis ugieia men paragignomai nosos de apogignomai ou kai su oiomai outws
english_head: So I should think, for the preservation of his passengers. Quite right. And now, for what is the good counsel of which you speak? For the better management and preservation of the city. And what is it that becomes present or absent when we get this better management and preservation? If, for example, you should ask me, What is it that becomes present or absent when the body is better managed and preserved?—I should reply, Health becomes present, and disease absent. Do not you think so too?

greek_head: Ναί Καὶ εἴ μ' αὖ ἔροιο Τίνος δὲ παραγιγνομένου
greek_tokens: nai kai ei egw au eromai tis de paragignomai ameinwn omma wsautws eipon an ostis oyis men paragignomai tufloths de apogignomai kai ous de kwfoths men apogignomai akoh de eggignomai beltiwn te gignomai kai ameinwn qerapeuw orqos tis de dh polis tis paragignomai kai apogignomai beltiwn te gignomai kai ameinwn qerapeuw kai dioikew
english_head: Yes. And if, again, you asked me, What becomes present in a better condition of the eyes?—I should answer in just the same way, Sight becomes present, and blindness absent. So, in the case of the ears, deafness is caused to be absent, and hearing to be present, when they are improved and getting better treatment. Correct. Well then, what is it that becomes present or absent when a state is improved and has better treatment and management?

greek_head: Ἐμοὶ μὲν δοκεῖ ὦ Σώκρατες ὅταν φιλία μὲν αὐτοῖς γίγνηται πρὸς ἀλλήλους τὸ μισεῖν δὲ καὶ στασιάζειν
greek_tokens: egw men dokew eimi swkraths otan filios men autos gignomai pros allhlwn o misew de kai stasiazw apogignomai ara oun filios legw omonoia h dixonoia omonoia dia tinw oun texnh omonoew o polis peri ariqmos dia o ariqmhtikos tis de e idioomai ou dia o autos nai oukoun kai autos eautou ekastos nai dia tis de texnh ekastos autos eautou omonoew
english_head: To my mind, Socrates, friendship with one another will be there, while hatred and faction will be absent. Now, by friendship do you mean agreement or disagreement? Agreement. And what art is it that causes states to agree about numbers? Arithmetic. And what of individuals? Is it not the same art? Yes. And it makes each single person agree with himself? Yes. And what art makes each of us agree with himself

greek_head: περὶ σπιθαμῆς καὶ πήχεος ὁπότερον μεῖζον οὐ διὰ τὴν μετρητικήν
greek_tokens: peri spiqamh kai phxus opoteros megas ou dia o metrhtikos tis meis oukoun kai e idioomai allhlwn kai o polis nai tis de peri staqmaomai ou wsautws fhmi os de dh su legw omonoia tis eimi kai peri o kai tis autos texnh paraskeuazw kai ara o polis autos kai idiwths autos te pros eautou kai pros allos eoika ge su tis oun eimi mh kamnw apokrinw alla
english_head: as to which is the longer, a span or a cubit? Is it not mensuration? Of course. And it makes both individuals and states agree with each other? Yes. And what about the balance? Is it not the same here too? It is. Then what is that agreement of which you speak, and about what? And what art secures it? And is it the same in an individual as in a state, when one agrees with oneself and with another? Most likely. Well, what is it? Do not flag in your answers,

greek_head: προθυμοῦ εἰπεῖν Ἐγὼ μὲν οἶμαι φιλίαν τε λέγειν καὶ ὁμόνοιαν
greek_tokens: proqumeomai eipon egw men oimh filios te legw kai omonoia os pathr te uios filos omonoew kai mhthr kai adelfos adelfos kai gunh andris oiomai an oun eimi alkibiadhs anhr gunh peri talasiourgia dunamai omonoew o mh efisthmi o efisthmi ou dhta oude ge deomai oudeis gunaikeios gar outos ge maqhma nai
english_head: but do your best to tell me. I suppose I mean the friendship and agreement that you find when a father and mother love their son, and between brother and brother, and husband and wife. Then do you suppose, Alcibiades, that a husband can possibly agree with his wife about woolwork, when he does not understand it, and she does? Oh, no. Nor has he any need, since that is a woman’s pursuit. Yes.

greek_head: Τί δέ γυνὴ ἀνδρὶ περὶ ὁπλιτικῆς δύναιτ' ἂν ὁμονοεῖν μὴ μαθοῦσα
greek_tokens: tis de gunh andris peri oplitikos dunamai an omonoew mh manqanw ou dhta andreios gar outos ge isos au fhmi an eimi egw eimi ara o men gunaikeios o de andreia maqhma kata o sos logos pws de ou ou ara en ge outos eimi omonoia gunh pros anhr ou oude ara filios eiper o filios omonoia eimi ou fainw h ara o gunh o eautou prassw ou filew upo o androw
english_head: Or again, could a woman agree with a man about soldiering, when she has not learnt it? Oh, no. Because, I expect you will say again, that is a man’s affair. I would. Then, by your account, there are some pursuits belonging to women, and some to men? Of course. So in these, at any rate, there is no agreement between men and women. No. And hence no friendship either, if, as we said, friendship is agreement. Apparently not. So women are not loved by men, in so far as they do their own work.

greek_head: Οὐκ ἔοικεν Οὐδ' ἄρα οἱ ἄνδρες ὑπὸ τῶν γυναικῶν ᾗ τὰ
greek_tokens: ou eoika oude ara e anhr upo o gunaikow h o eautou ou oude eu ara outos oikew o polis otan o eautou ekastos prassw oimh egw eimi swkraths pws legw filios mh pareimi eis fhmi eggignomai eu oikew o polis allos de ou alla egw dokew kai kata outos autos filios eggignomai ostis o eautou ekateros prassw
english_head: It seems not. Nor are men by women, in so far as they do theirs. No. And states, therefore, are not well ordered in so far as each person does his own business? I think they are, Socrates. How can you say that? Without the presence of friendship, which we say must be there if states are well ordered, as otherwise they are not? But it seems to me that friendship arises among them just on that account—that each of the two parties does its own business.

greek_head: Οὐκ ἄρτι γε νῦν δὲ πῶς αὖ λέγεις ὁμονοίας μὴ ἐγγιγνομένης φιλία ἐγγίγνεται ἢ οἷόν θ' ὁμόνοιαν ἐγγίγνεσθαι
greek_tokens: ou arti ge nun de pws au legw omonoia mh eggignomai filios eggignomai h oios su omonoia eggignomai peri outos os e men oida peri outos e de ou adunatos dikaios de prassw h adikos otan o eautou ekastos prassw dikaios pws gar ou o dikaios oun prassw en o polis o poliths filios ou eggignomai pros allhlwn anagkh au egw dokew eimi eimi swkraths
english_head: It was not so a moment since: but now, what do you mean this time? Does friendship arise where there is no agreement? And is it possible that agreement should arise where some know about the business, but others do not? Impossible. And are they doing what is just or unjust, when each man does his own business? What is just, of course. And when the citizens do what is just in the city, does not friendship arise among them? Again I think that must be so, Socrates. Then whatever do you mean by that

greek_head: Τίνα οὖν ποτε λέγεις τὴν φιλίαν ἢ ὁμόνοιαν περὶ ἧς δεῖ ἡμᾶς σοφούς τε εἶναι καὶ εὐβούλους ἵνα ἀγαθοὶ
greek_tokens: tis oun pote legw o filios h omonoia peri eis deomai egw sofos te eimi kai euboulos ina agaqos anhr eimi ou gar dunamai manqanw oute ostis oute en ostis tote men gar en o autos fainw eneimi tote de ou ws ek o sew logos alla ma o qeos eimi swkraths oude autos oida ostis legos kinduneuw de kai pala lanqanw emautou aisxros exw alla xrh qarsew ei men gar autos aisqanomai pasxw
english_head: about which we must be wise and well-advised in order that we may be good men? For I am unable to learn either what it is, or in whom; since it appears that the same persons sometimes have it, and sometimes not, by your account. Well, by Heaven, Socrates, I do not even know what I mean myself, and I fear that for some time past I have lived unawares in a disgraceful condition. But you must take heart. For had you perceived your plight

greek_head: πεντηκονταετής χαλεπὸν ἂν ἦν σοι ἐπιμεληθῆναι σαυτοῦ νῦν δ' ἣν ἔχεις ἡλικίαν αὕτη ἐστὶν ἐν ᾗ δεῖ αὐτὸ
greek_tokens: penthkontaeths xalepos an eimi su epimeleomai sautou nun de os exis hlikia outos eimi en h deomai autos aisqanomai tis oun o aisqanomai xrh poiew eimi swkraths apokrinw o erwtaw eimi alkibiadhs kai ean outos poiew an qeos eqelw ei tis deomai kai o emew manteia pisteuw su te agos beltiwn exw eimi outos eneka ge o egw apokrinw ferw dh tis eimi o eautou epimeleomai mh pollakis
english_head: at fifty, it would be hard for you to take pains with yourself; whereas here you are at the time of life when one ought to perceive it. Then what should one do on perceiving it, Socrates? Answer the questions asked, Alcibiades: only do that, and with Heaven’s favor—if we are to put any trust in my divination—you and I shall both be in better case. That shall be, so far as my answering can avail. Come then, what is taking pains over oneself—

greek_head: λάθωμεν οὐχ ἡμῶν αὐτῶν ἐπιμελούμενοι οἰόμενοι δέ καὶ πότ' ἄρα αὐτὸ ποιεῖ ἅνθρωπος ἆρ' ὅταν τῶν αὑτοῦ
greek_tokens: lanqanw ou egw autos epimeleomai oiomai de kai pote ara autos poiew anqrwpos ara otan o eautou epimeleomai tote kai eautou egw goun dokew tis de podow anqrwpos pote epimeleomai ara otan ekeinos epimeleomai o eimi o podow ou manqanw kalew de tis xeir oios daktulion eimi ostis an allos o o anqrwpos fhmi h daktulos ou dhta oukoun kai pous upodhma o autos tropos nai kai imation kai strwma o allos swma omoios
english_head: for we may perchance be taking, unawares, no pains over ourselves, though we think we are—and when does a man actually do it? Does he take pains over himself at the same time as over his own things? I at least believe so. Well now, when does a man take pains over his feet? Is it when he takes pains over what belongs to his feet? I do not understand. Is there anything you can name as belonging to the hand? For instance, does a ring belong to any other part of a man but the finger? No, indeed. And

greek_head: Ναί Ἆρ' οὖν ὅταν ὑποδημάτων ἐπιμελώμεθα τότε ποδῶν
greek_tokens: nai ara oun otan upodhma epimeleomai tote podow epimeleomai ou panu manqanw eimi swkraths tis de eimi alkibiadhs orqos epimeleomai kalew tis ostis pragma egw ara oun otan tis tis beltiwn poiew tote orqos legw epimeleia nai tis oun texnh upodhma beltiwn poiew skutikos skutikos ara upodhma epimeleomai
english_head: Yes. Now when we take pains over our shoes, we take pains over our feet? I do not quite understand, Socrates. Well, but, Alcibiades, you speak of taking proper pains over this or that matter, do you not? I do. And do you call it proper pains when someone makes a thing better? Yes. Then what art makes shoes better? Shoe-making. So by shoe-making we take pains over our shoes?

greek_head: Ναί Ἦ καὶ ποδὸς σκυτικῇ ἢ ἐκείνῃ ᾗ πόδας βελτίους
greek_tokens: nai eimi kai pous skutikos h ekeinos h pous beltiwn poiew ekeinos beltiwn de pous ou hper kai o allos swma egw dokew outos de ou gumnastikos malista gumnastikos men ara pous epimeleomai skutikos de o o pous panu ge kai gumnastikos men xeir daktulioglufia de o o xeir nai kai gumnastikos men swma ufantikos de kai o
english_head: Yes. And over our foot too by shoe-making? Or by that art whereby we make feet better? By that art. And is it not the same one for making our feet as for making the whole body better? I think so. And is not that gymnastic? Certainly. So by gymnastic we take pains over our foot, but by shoe-making over what belongs to our foot? Quite so. And by gymnastic over our hands, but by ring-engraving over what belongs to the hand? Yes. And by gymnastic over the body, but by weaving

greek_head: ἄλλαις τῶν τοῦ σώματος Παντάπασι μὲν οὖν
greek_tokens: allos o o swma pantapasi men oun allh men ara texnh autos ekastos epimeleomai allh de o autos fainw ou ara otan o sautou epimeleomai sautou epimeleomai oudamos ou gar o autos texnh ws eoika h tis an eautou te epimeleomai kai o eautou ou fainw ferw dh poa pote an egw autos epimeleomai ou exw legw
english_head: and the rest over what belongs to the body? Absolutely so. Then for taking pains over a thing itself and over what belongs to it we use different arts. Apparently. So when you take pains over your belongings you are not taking pains over yourself. Not at all. For the arts, it seems, that one used for taking pains over oneself and over one’s belongings would not be the same. Apparently not. Come then, whatever kind of art can we use for taking pains over ourselves? I cannot say.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ τοσόνδε γε ὡμολόγηται ὅτι οὐχ ᾗ ἂν τῶν ἡμετέρων καὶ ὁτιοῦν βέλτιον ποιοῖμεν ἀλλ' ᾗ ἡμᾶς αὐτούς
greek_tokens: alla tososde ge omologew ostis ou h an o hmeteros kai otioun beltiwn poiew alla h egw autos alhqhs legw eimi oun gignwskw an pote tis texnh upodhma beltiwn poiew mh oida upodhma adunatos oude ge tis texnh daktulios beltiwn poiew agnoew daktulion alhqhs tis de tis texnh beltiwn poiew autos ara an pote gignwskw agnoew tis pote eimi autos
english_head: Well, so much at least has been admitted, that it is not one which would help us to make a single one of our possessions better, but one which would help to make ourselves so? That is true. Now, should we ever have known what art makes a shoe better, if we had not known a shoe? Impossible. Nor could we know what art makes rings better, if we had no cognizance of a ring. True. Well then, could we ever know what art makes the man himself better, if we were ignorant of what we are ourselves?

greek_head: Ἀδύνατον Πότερον οὖν δὴ ῥᾴδιον τυγχάνει τὸ γνῶναι ἑαυτόν
greek_tokens: adunatos poteros oun dh radios tugxanw o gignwskw eautou kai tis eimi faulos o outos anatiqhmi eis o en puqoi naos h xalepos tis kai ou pas egw men eimi swkraths pollakis men dokew pas eimi pollakis de pagxalepos alla eimi alkibiadhs eite radios eite mh eimi omws ge egw wde exis gignwskw men autos taxa an gignwskw o epimeleia egw autos agnoew de ou an pote eimi outos
english_head: Impossible. Well, and is it an easy thing to know oneself, and was it a mere scamp who inscribed these words on the temple at Delphi; or is it a hard thing, and not a task for anybody? I have often thought, Socrates, that it was for anybody; but often, too, that it was very hard. But, Alcibiades, whether it is easy or not, here is the fact for us all the same: if we have that knowledge, we are like to know what pains to take over ourselves; but if we have it not, we never can. That is so.

greek_head: Φέρε δή τίν' ἂν τρόπον εὑρεθείη αὐτὸ ταὐτό οὕτω μὲν γὰρ ἂν τάχ' εὕροιμεν τί ποτ' ἐσμὲν αὐτοί τούτου δ' ἔτι
greek_tokens: ferw dh tinw an tropos euriskw autos autos outws men gar an taxa euriskw tis pote eimi autos outos de eti eimi en agnoia adunatos pou orqos legw exw oun pros zeus o dialegw su nun allos tis h egw nai oukoun kai egw sos nai swkratew ara eimi o dialegw panu ge alkibiadhs de o akouw nai oukoun logos dialegw o swkratew
english_head: Come then, in what way can the same-in-itself be discovered? For thus we may discover what we are ourselves; whereas if we remain in ignorance of it we must surely fail. Rightly spoken. Steady, then, in Heaven’s name! To whom are you talking now? To me, are you not? Yes. And I in turn to you ? Yes. Then the talker is Socrates? To be sure. And the hearer, Alcibiades? Yes. And Socrates uses speech in talking?

greek_head: Τί μήν Τὸ δὲ διαλέγεσθαι καὶ τὸ λόγῳ χρῆσθαι ταὐτόν που
greek_tokens: tis meis o de dialegw kai o logos xraomai tautos pou kalew panu ge o de xraomai kai os xraomai ou allos pws legw wsper skutotomos temnw pou tomaw kai smilh kai allos organon nai oukoun allos men o temnw kai xraomai allos de os temnw xraomai pws gar ou ara oun outws kai os o kiqarisths kiqarizw kai autos o kiqarisths allos an eaw nai
english_head: Of course. And you call talking and using speech the same thing, I suppose. To be sure. But the user and the thing he uses are different, are they not? How do you mean? For instance, I suppose a shoemaker uses a round tool, and a square one, and others, when he cuts. Yes. And the cutter and user is quite different from what he uses in cutting? Of course. And in the same way what the harper uses in harping will be different from the harper himself? Yes. Well then, that is what I was asking just n

greek_head: Τοῦτο τοίνυν ἀρτίως ἠρώτων εἰ ὁ χρώμενος καὶ ᾧ χρῆται ἀεὶ δοκεῖ ἕτερον εἶναι
greek_tokens: outos toinun artios erwtaw ei o xraomai kai os xraomai aei dokew eteros eimi dokew tis oun fhmi o skutotomos temnw organon monos h kai xeir kai xeir xraomai ara kai outos nai eimi kai o ofqalmos xraomai skutotomew nai o de xraomai kai os xraomai eteros omologew nai eteros ara skutotomos kai kiqarisths xeir kai
english_head: and what he uses are always, in your opinion, two different things. They are. Then what are we to say of the shoemaker? Does he cut with his tools only, or with his hands as well? With his hands as well. So he uses these also? Yes. Does he use his eyes, too, in his shoe-making? Yes. And we admit that the user and what he uses are different things? Yes. Then the shoemaker and the harper are different from

greek_head: ὀφθαλμῶν οἷς ἐργάζονται Φαίνεται
greek_tokens: ofqalmos os ergazomai fainw oukoun kai pas o swma xraomai anqrwpos panu ge eteros de eimi o te xraomai kai os xraomai nai eteros ara anqrwpos eimi o swma o eautou eoika tis pote oun o anqrwpos ou exw legw exis men oun ostis ge o o swma xraomai nai
english_head: the hands and eyes that they use for their work? Apparently. And man uses his whole body too? To be sure. And we said that the user and what he uses are different? Yes. So man is different from his own body? It seems so. Then whatever is man? I cannot say. Oh, but you can—that he is the user of the body. Yes.

greek_head: Ἦ οὖν ἄλλο τι χρῆται αὐτῷ ἢ ψυχή Οὐκ ἄλλο
greek_tokens: eimi oun allos tis xraomai autos h yuxh ou allos oukoun arxw nai kai meis ode ge oimh oudeis an allos oiomai o poios mh ou treis eis ge tis eimi o anqrwpos tinw yuxh h swma h sunamfoteroi o oloc outos tis meis alla meis autos ge o o swma arxw omologew anqrwpos eimi
english_head: And the user of it must be the soul? It must. And ruler? Yes. Now, here is a remark from which no one, I think, can dissent. What is it? That man must be one of three things. What things? Soul, body, or both together as one whole. Very well. But yet we have admitted that what actually rules the body is man?

greek_head: Ὡμολογήσαμεν Ἆρ' οὖν σῶμα αὐτὸ αὑτοῦ ἄρχει
greek_tokens: omologew ara oun swma autos eautou arxw oudamos arxw gar autos eipon nai ou an dh outos ge eaw o zhtew ou eoika alla ara o sunamfoteroi o swma arxw kai eimi dh outos anqrwpos isos dhta pas ge hkistos mh gar sunarxw o eteros oudeis pou mhxanh o sunamfoteroi arxw orqos
english_head: We have. And does the body rule itself? By no means. Because we have said that it is ruled. Yes. Then that cannot be what we are seeking. It seems not. Well then, does the combination of the two rule the body, so that we are to regard this as man? Perhaps it is. The unlikeliest thing in the world: for if one of the two does not share in the rule, it is quite inconceivable that the combination of the two can be ruling. You are right.

greek_head: Ἐπειδὴ δ' οὔτε σῶμα οὔτε τὸ συναμφότερόν ἐστιν ἄνθρωπος λείπεται οἶμαι ἢ μηδὲν αὔτ' εἶναι ἢ εἴπερ τί ἐστι
greek_tokens: epei de oute swma oute o sunamfoteroi eimi anqrwpos leipw oimh h mhdeis autos eimi h eiper tis eimi mhdeis allos o anqrwpos sumbainw h yuxh komidh men oun eti oun tis safhs deomai apodeiknumi su ostis o yuxh eimi anqrwpos ma dios alla ikanos egw dokew exw ei de ge mh akribhs alla kai metrios ecarkew egw akribhs men gar tote eimi otan euriskw o nundi
english_head: But since neither the body nor the combination of the two is man, we are reduced, I suppose, to this: either man is nothing at all, or if something, he turns out to be nothing else than soul. Precisely so. Well, do you require some yet clearer proof that the soul is man? No, I assure you: I think it is amply proved. And if it is tolerably, though not exactly, we are content; exact knowledge will be ours later,

greek_head: παρήλθομεν διὰ τὸ πολλῆς εἶναι σκέψεως Τί τοῦτο
greek_tokens: parerxomai dia o polus eimi skeyis tis outos o arti outws pws erw ostis proteros skepteos eaw autos o autos nun de anti o autos autos ekastos skeptomai ostis eimi kai isos ecarkew ou gar pou kurios ge oudeis an egw autos fhmi h o yuxh ou dhta oukoun kalos exis outws nomizw egw kai sos prosomilew allhlwn o logos xraomai o yuxw pros o yuxh
english_head: when we have discovered the thing that we passed over just now because it would involve much consideration. What is that? The point suggested in that remark a moment ago, that we should first consider the same-in-itself; but so far, instead of the same, we have been considering what each single thing is in itself. And perhaps we shall be satisfied with that: for surely we cannot say that anything has more absolute possession of ourselves than the soul. No, indeed. And it is proper to take the vi

greek_head: Πάνυ μὲν οὖν Τοῦτ' ἄρα ἦν ὃ καὶ ὀλίγῳ ἔμπροσθεν εἴπομεν
greek_tokens: panu men oun outos ara eimi o kai oligos emprosqen eipon ostis swkratew alkibiadhs dialegw logos xraomai ou pros o sos proswpon ws eoika alla pros o alkibiadhs poiew o logos outos de eimi o yuxh egw dokew yuxh ara egw keleuw gnwrizw o epitassw gignwskw eautou
english_head: Well, that is just what we suggested a little while ago—that Socrates, in using words to talk with Alcibiades, is holding speech, not with your face, it would seem, but with Alcibiades—that is, with his soul. I believe so. Then he who enjoins a knowledge of oneself bids us become acquainted with the soul.

greek_head: Ἔοικεν Ὅστις ἄρα τῶν τοῦ σώματός τι γιγνώσκει τὰ αὑτοῦ
greek_tokens: eoika ostis ara o o swma tis gignwskw o eautou alla ou eautou gignwskw outws oudeis ara o iatros eautou gignwskw kaqa osos iatros oude o paidotribhs kaqa osos paidotribhs ou eoika polus ara deon e gewrgos kai e allos dhmiourgos gignwskw eautou oude gar o eautou outos ge ws eoika alla eti prosw o eautou kata ge o
english_head: So it seems. And anyone who gets to know something belonging to the body knows the things that are his, but not himself. That is so. Then no physician, in so far as he is a physician, knows himself, nor does any trainer, in so far as he is a trainer. It seems not. And farmers, and craftsmen generally, are far from knowing themselves. For these people, it would seem, do not even know their own things, but only things still more remote than their own things, in respect of the arts which they follo

greek_head: τέχνας ἃς ἔχουσιν τὰ γὰρ τοῦ σώματος γιγνώσκουσιν οἷς τοῦτο θεραπεύεται
greek_tokens: texnh os exw o gar o swma gignwskw os outos qerapeuw alhqhs legw ei ara swfrosunos eimi o eautou gignwskw oudeis outos swfrwn kata o texnh ou egw dokew dia outos dh kai banausos outos o texnh dokew eimi kai ou anhr agaqos maqhma panu men oun oukoun palin ostis au swma qerapeuw o eautou alla ou eautou qerapeuw kinduneuw ostis de ge o xrhma oute eautou oute o
english_head: but the things of the body, with which it is tended. That is true. So if knowing oneself is temperance, none of these people is temperate in respect of his art. None, I agree. And that is why these arts are held to be sordid, and no acquirements for a good man. Quite so. Then once again, whoever tends his body tends his own things, but not himself? It looks rather like it. But whoever tends his money tends neither himself nor

greek_head: ἑαυτοῦ ἀλλ' ἔτι πορρωτέρω τῶν ἑαυτοῦ Ἔμοιγε δοκεῖ
greek_tokens: eautou alla eti prosw o eautou egw dokew ou o eautou ara eti prassw o xrhmatisths orqos ei ara tis gignomai erasths o alkibiadhs swma ou alkibiadhs ara arazw alla tis o alkibiadhs alhqhs legw ostis de su o yuxazw eraomai anagkh fainw ek o logos oukoun o men o swma su era epei lhgw anqew apeimi oixomai fainw
english_head: his own things, but only things yet more remote than his own things? I agree. So that the money-maker has ceased to do his own business. Correct. And if anyone is found to be a lover of Alcibiades’ body, he has fallen in love, not with Alcibiades, but with something belonging to Alcibiades? That is true. Your lover is rather he who loves your soul? He must be, apparently, by our argument. And he who loves your body quits you, and is gone, as soon as its bloom is over? Apparently.

greek_head: Ὁ δέ γε τῆς ψυχῆς ἐρῶν οὐκ ἄπεισιν ἕως ἂν ἐπὶ τὸ βέλτιον ἴῃ
greek_tokens: o de ge o yuxazw era ou apeimi ews an epi o beltiwn eimi eoika ge oukoun egw eimi o ou apeimi alla paramenw lhgw o swma o allos aperxomai eu ge poa eimi swkraths kai mhde aperxomai proqumeomai toinun ostis kalos eimi alla proqumeomai
english_head: Whereas he who loves your soul will not quit you so long as it makes for what is better? So it seems. And I am he who does not quit you, but remains with you when your body’s prime is over, and the rest have departed. Yes, and I am glad of it, Socrates, and hope you will not go. Then you must endeavor to be as handsome as you can. Well, I shall endeavor. You see how you stand: Alcibiades,

greek_head: Ὡς οὕτω γέ σοι ἔχει οὔτ' ἐγένεθ' ὡς ἔοικεν Ἀλκιβιάδῃ τῷ Κλεινίου ἐραστὴς οὔτ' ἔστιν ἀλλ' ἢ εἷς
greek_tokens: ws outws ge su exis oute gignomai ws eoika alkibiadhs o kleinihs erasths oute eimi alla h eis monos kai outos agaphtos swkratew o swfroniskos kai fainareth alhqhs oukoun fhmi mikros fqanw egw proserxomai su epei proteros an egw proserxomai boulomai punqanomai dia ostis monos ou aperxomai eimi gar outws outos toinun aitios ostis monos erasths eimi sos e de allos o sew o de sos lhgw oraw su de arxw anqew
english_head: the son of Cleinias, it seems, neither had nor has any lover except one only, and that a cherished one, Socrates, the son of Sophroniscus and Phaenarete. True. And you said that I only just anticipated you in coming to you, for otherwise you would have come to me first for the purpose of inquiring why I am the only one who does not leave you? Yes, that was so. Then the reason was that I was the only lover of you, whereas the rest were lovers of what is yours; and that is losing its charm,

greek_head: καὶ νῦν γε ἂν μὴ διαφθαρῇς ὑπὸ τοῦ Ἀθηναίων δήμου καὶ αἰσχίων γένῃ οὐ μή σε ἀπολίπω τοῦτο γὰρ δὴ μάλιστα
greek_tokens: kai nun ge an mh diafqeirw upo o aqhnaia dhmos kai aisxos gignomai ou mh su apoleipw outos gar dh malista egw fobew mh dhmerasths egw gignomai diafqeirw polus gar hdh kai agaqos autos pasxw aqhnaia euproswpos gar o o megalhtwr dhmos erexqeus alla apodunw xrh autos qeaomai eulabeomai oun o eulabeia os egw legos tis
english_head: while you are beginning to bloom. So now, if you are not blighted and deformed by the Athenian people, I shall never forsake you. For my chiefest fear is of your being blighted by becoming a lover of the people, since many a good Athenian has come to that ere now. For fair of face is the people of great-hearted Erechtheus;Hom. Il. 2.547 but you should get a view of it stripped: so take the precaution that I recommend. What is it?

greek_head: Γύμνασαι πρῶτον ὦ μακάριε καὶ μάθε ἃ δεῖ μαθόντα ἰέναι ἐπὶ τὰ τῆς πόλεως πρότερον δὲ μή ἵν' ἀλεξιφάρμακα
greek_tokens: gumnazw proteros eimi makarios kai manqanw o deomai manqanw eimi epi o o polis proteros de mh ina alecifarmakon exw eimi kai mhdeis paqh deinos eu egw dokew legw eimi swkraths alla peiraw echgeomai ostis an tropos epimeleomai egw autos oukoun tosoutos men egw eis o prosqen peraw o gar eimi epieikhs omologew fobew de mh outos sfallw lanqanw eteros tis epimeleomai alla ou egw eimi outos
english_head: Exercise yourself first, my wonderful friend, in learning what you ought to know before entering on politics; you must wait till you have learnt, in order that you may be armed with an antidote and so come to no harm. Your advice seems to me good, Socrates; but try to explain in what way we can take pains over ourselves. Well, we have made one step in advance; for there is a pretty fair agreement now as to what we are, whereas we were afraid we might fail of this and take pains, without knowing

greek_head: Καὶ μετὰ τοῦτο δὴ ὅτι ψυχῆς ἐπιμελητέον καὶ εἰς τοῦτο βλεπτέον
greek_tokens: kai meta outos dh ostis yuxazw epimelhteon kai eis outos blepteon dhlos swma de kai xrhma o epimeleia eteros paradoteos tis meis tinw oun an tropos gignwskw autos enarghs epei outos gignwskw ws eoika kai egw autos gignwskw ara pros qea eu legw e nundi mimnhskw o delfikos gramma ou sunihmi o poios tis dianoeomai legw eimi swkraths
english_head: And the next step, we see, is to take care of the soul, and look to that. Clearly. While handing over to others the care of our bodies and our coffers. Quite so. Then how shall we obtain the most certain knowledge of it? For if we know that, it seems we shall know ourselves also. In Heaven’s name, do we fail to comprehend the wise words of the Delphic inscription, which we mentioned just now? With what intent do you say that, Socrates?

greek_head: Ἐγώ σοι φράσω ὅ γε ὑποπτεύω λέγειν καὶ συμβουλεύειν ἡμῖν τοῦτο τὸ γράμμα κινδυνεύει γὰρ οὐδὲ πολλαχοῦ
greek_tokens: egw su frazw o ge upopteuw legw kai sumbouleuw egw outos o gramma kinduneuw gar oude pollaxou eimi paradeigma autos alla kata o oyis monos pws outos legw skopaw kai su ei egw o omma wsper anqrwpos sumbouleuw eipon ide sautou pws an upolambanw tis parainew ara ou eis outos blepw eis o blepw o ofqalmos mellw eautou eidon dhlos ennoew dh eis tis blepw o eimi ekeinos
english_head: I will tell you what I suspect to be the real advice which the inscription gives us. I rather think there are not many illustrations of it to be found, but only in the case of sight. What do you mean by that? Consider in your turn: suppose that, instead of speaking to a man, it said to the eye of one of us, as a piece of advice See thyself, how should we apprehend the meaning of the admonition? Would it not be, that the eye should look at that by looking at which it would see itself? Clearly. Th

greek_tokens: te oraw ama an kai egw autos dhlos dh eimi swkraths ostis eis katoptron te kai o toioutos orqos legw oukoun kai o ofqalmos os oraw eneimi tis o toioutos panu ge ennoew oun ostis o emblepw eis o
english_head: we can see both it and ourselves. Why, clearly, Socrates, mirrors and things of that sort. Quite right. And there is also something of that sort in the eye that we see with? To be sure. And have you observed that the face of the person who looks into another’s eye is shown in the optic confronting him,

greek_tokens: alhqhs legw ofqalmos ara ei mellw eidon eautou eis ofqalmos autos blepteon kai o omma eis ekeinos o topos en os tugxanw o ofqalmos areth eggignomai eimi de outos pou oyis outws ara oun eimi filos alkibiadhs kai yuxh ei mellw gignwskw eautou eis yuxh autos blepteon kai malista eis outos autos o topos en os eggignomai o yuxazw areth sofia kai eis allos os outos tugxanw omoios eimi egw dokew eimi swkraths
english_head: That is true. Then an eye viewing another eye, and looking at the most perfect part of it, the thing wherewith it sees, will thus see itself. Apparently. But if it looks at any other thing in man or at anything in nature but what resembles this, it will not see itself. That is true. Then if an eye is to see itself, it must look at an eye, and at that region of the eye in which the virtue of an eye is found to occur; and this, I presume, is sight. That is so. And if the soul too, my dear Alcibiad

english_head: And can we find any part of the soul that we can call more divine than this, which is the seat of knowledge and thought? We cannot. Then this part of her resembles God, and whoever looks at this, and comes to know all that is divine, will gain thereby the best knowledge of himself. Apparently. And self-knowledge we admitted to be temperance. To be sure. So if we have no knowledge of ourselves and no temperance, shall we be able to know our own belongings, good or evil? How can that be, Socrates?

greek_head: Ἀδύνατον γὰρ ἴσως σοι φαίνεται μὴ γιγνώσκοντα Ἀλκιβιάδην τὰ Ἀλκιβιάδου γιγνώσκειν ὅτι Ἀλκιβιάδου
greek_tokens: adunatos gar isos su fainw mh gignwskw alkibiadhs o alkibiadhs gignwskw ostis alkibiadhs eimi adunatos mentoi nh dios oude ara o hmeteros ostis hmeteros ei mhde egw autos pws gar ei de ara mhde o hmeteros oude o o hmeteros ou fainw ou ara panu tis orqos omologew omologew arti eimi tis e eautou men ou gignwskw o de eautou allos de o o eautou eoika gar pas outos eimi
english_head: For I expect it seems impossible to you that without knowing Alcibiades you should know that the belongings of Alcibiades are in fact his. Impossible indeed, upon my word. Nor could we know that our belongings are ours if we did not even know ourselves? How could we? And so, if we did not so much as know our belongings, we could not know the belongings of our belongings either? Apparently not. Then we were not quite correct in admitting just now that there are people who, without knowing themsel

greek_head: κατιδεῖν ἑνός τε καὶ μιᾶς τέχνης αὑτόν τὰ αὑτοῦ τὰ τῶν ἑαυτοῦ
greek_tokens: kateidon eis te kai eis texnh eautou o eautou o o eautou kinduneuw ostis de o eautou agnoew kai o o allos pou an agnoew kata tautos tis meis oukoun ei o o allos kai o o polis agnohsis anagkh ou ara an gignomai o toioutos anhr politikos ou dhta ou meis oude oikonomikos ge
english_head: himself, his belongings, and the belongings of his belongings. It looks like it. And anyone who is ignorant of his belongings will be similarly ignorant, I suppose, of the belongings of others. Quite so. And if ignorant of others’ affairs, he will be ignorant also of the affairs of states. He must be. Then such a man can never be a statesman. No, indeed. No, nor an economist either.

greek_head: Οὐ δῆτα Οὐδέ γε εἴσεται ὅτι πράττει
greek_tokens: ou dhta oude ge eimi ostis prassw ou gar oun o de mh eidoi ou amartanw panu ge ecamartanw de ou kakos prassw idios te kai dhmosios pws de ou kakos de prassw ou aqlios sfodra ge tis de os outos prassw kai outos ou ara oios te ean mh tis swfrwn kai agaqos eimi eudaimwn eimi
english_head: No, indeed. Nor will he know what he is doing. No, I agree. And will not he who does not know make mistakes? To be sure. And when he makes mistakes, will he not do ill both in private and in public? Of course. And doing ill he will be wretched? Yes, very. And what of those for whom he is doing so? They will be wretched also. Then it is impossible to be happy if one is not temperate and good. Impossible.

greek_head: Οὐχ οἷόν τε Οἱ ἄρα κακοὶ τῶν ἀνθρώπων ἄθλιοι
greek_tokens: ou oios te e ara kakos o anqrwpos aqlios sfodra ge ou ara oude o ploutew aqlioths apallassw alla o swfronew fainw ou ara teixos oude trihrhs oude newrhs deomai o polis eimi alkibiadhs ei mellw eudaimonew oude plhqos oude megeqos aneu aretaw ou mentoi ei dh mellw o o polis prassw orqos kai
english_head: So it is the bad men who are wretched. Yes, very. And hence it is not he who has made himself rich that is relieved of wretchedness, but he who has made himself temperate. Apparently. So it is not walls or warships or arsenals that cities need, Alcibiades, if they are to be happy, nor numbers, nor size, without virtue. No, indeed. And if you are to manage the city’s affairs properly and honorably, you must impart virtue to the citizens. Of course.

greek_head: καλῶς ἀρετῆς σοι μεταδοτέον τοῖς πολίταις Πῶς γὰρ οὔ
greek_tokens: kalos aretaw su metadoteon o poliths pws gar ou dunamai de an tis metadidwmi o mh exw kai pws autos ara sos proteros kthteos areth kai allos os mellw mh idios monos eautou te kai o eautou arxw kai epimeleomai alla polis kai o o polis alhqhs legw ou ara ecousia su oude arxh paraskeuasteos sautou poiew ostis an boulomai oude o polis alla dikaiosunh kai swfrosunos fainw
english_head: But could one possibly impart a thing that one had not? How, indeed? Then you or anyone else who is to be governor and curator, not merely of himself and his belongings in private, but of the state and its affairs, must first acquire virtue himself. That is true. Hence it is not licence or authority for doing what one pleases that you have to secure to yourself or the state, but justice and temperance. Apparently.

greek_head: Δικαίως μὲν γὰρ πράττοντες καὶ σωφρόνως σύ τε καὶ ἡ πόλις θεοφιλῶς πράξετε
greek_tokens: dikaios men gar prassw kai swfrwn su te kai o polis qeofilhs prassw eoika ge kai osper ge en o prosqen legw eis o qew kai lampros oraw prassw fainw alla meis entauqa ge blepw su te autos kai o umeteros agaqos kaqoraw kai gignwskw nai oukoun orqos te kai eu prassw nai
english_head: For you and the state, if you act justly and temperately, will act so as to please God. Naturally. And, as we were saying in what went before, you will act with your eyes turned on what is divine and bright. Apparently. Well, and looking thereon you will behold and know both yourselves and your good. Yes. And so you will act aright and well? Yes.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ μὴν οὕτω γε πράττοντας ὑμᾶς ἐθέλω ἐγγυήσασθαι ἦ μὴν εὐδαιμονήσειν
greek_tokens: alla meis outws ge prassw su eqelw egguaw eimi meis eudaimonew asfalhs gar ei egguhths adikos de ge prassw eis o aqeos kai skoteinos blepw ws o eoika omoios outos prassw agnoew su autos eoika os gar an eimi filos alkibiadhs ecousia men eimi poiew o boulomai new de mh exis tis o eoika sumbainw idiwths h kai polis oios nosew ecousia eimi draw o
english_head: Well now, if you act in this way, I am ready to warrant that you must be happy. And I can rely on your warranty. But if you act unjustly, with your eyes on the godless and dark, the probability is that your acts will resemble these through your ignorance of yourselves. That is probable. For if a man, my dear Alcibiades, is at liberty to do what he pleases, but is lacking in mind, what is the probable result to him personally, or to the state as well? english_head: but with a despot’s power which prevents anyone from even reproving him—what will be the result? Will not his health, in all likelihood, be shattered? That is true. Again, in a ship, if a man were at liberty to do what he chose, but were devoid of mind and excellence in navigation, do you perceive what must happen to him and his fellow-sailors? I do: they must all perish. And in just the same way, if a state, or any office or authority, is lacking in excellence or virtue,

greek_head: ἐξουσίαις ἀπολειπομέναις ἀρετῆς ἕπεται τὸ κακῶς πράττειν Ἀνάγκη
greek_tokens: ecousia apoleipw aretaw epomai o kakos prassw anagkh ou ara turannis xrh eimi aristos alkibiadhs paraskeuazw oute eautou oute o polis ei mellw eudaimonew alla areth alhqhs legw prin de ge areth exw o arxw ameinwn upo o beltiwn h o arxw andris ou monos pais fainw oukoun o ge ameinwn kai kallion nai o de kallion prepwdhs
english_head: it will be overtaken by failure? It must. Then it is not despotic power, my admirable Alcibiades, that you ought to secure either to yourself or to the state, if you would be happy, but virtue. That is true. And before getting virtue, to be governed by a superior is better than to govern, for a man as well as a child. Apparently. And the better is also nobler? Yes. And the nobler more becoming? Of course.

greek_head: Πῶς δ' οὔ Πρέπει ἄρα τῷ κακῷ δουλεύειν ἄμεινον γάρ
greek_tokens: pws de ou prepw ara o kakazw douleuw ameinwn gar nai douloprephs ara o kakia fainw eleuqeroprephs de o areth nai oukoun feugw xrh eimi etairos o douloprepeia malista ge eimi swkraths aisqanomai de nun pws exis eleuqeroprephs h ou dokazw egw kai mala sfodra aisqanomai oistos oun pws apofeucis outos o peri sos nun ina mh onomazw autos epi kalos andris
english_head: Then it becomes a bad man to be a slave, since it is better. Yes. So vice is a thing that becomes a slave. Apparently. And virtue becomes a free man. Yes. And we should shun, my good friend, all slavishness? Most certainly, Socrates. And do you now perceive how you stand? Are you on the side of the free, or not? I think I perceive only too clearly. Then do you know how you may escape from the condition in which you now find yourself? Let us not give it a name, where a handsome person is concerne

greek_head: Ἔγωγε Πῶς
greek_tokens: egw pws ean boulomai su eimi swkraths ou kalos legw eimi alkibiadhs alla pws xrh legw ostis ean qeos eqelw legos dh kai pros outos mentoi ode legos ostis kinduneuw metaballw o sxhma eimi swkraths o men sos egw su de emos ou gar eimi opws ou paidagwgew su apo ode o hmeros su de upo egw paidagwgew
english_head: I do. How? If it be your wish, Socrates. That is not well said, Alcibiades. Well, what should I say? If it be God’s will. Then I say it. And yet I say this besides, that we are like to make a change in our parts, Socrates, so that I shall have yours and you mine. For from this day onward it must be the case that I am your attendant, and you have me always in attendance on you.

greek_head: Ὦ γενναῖε πελαργοῦ ἄρα ὁ ἐμὸς ἔρως οὐδὲν διοίσει εἰ παρὰ σοὶ ἐννεοττεύσας ἔρωτα ὑπόπτερον ὑπὸ
greek_tokens: eimi gennaios pelargos ara o emos eros oudeis diaferw ei para sos enneosseuw erws upopteros upo outos palin qerapeuw alla outws exis kai arxw ge enteuqen o dikaiosunh epimeleomai boulomai an su kai diatelew orrwdew de ou tis o sew fusis apistew alla o o polis oraw rwmh mh egw te kai sew krathsis
english_head: Ah, generous friend! So my love will be just like a stork; for after hatching a winged love in you it is to be cherished in return by its nestling. Well, that is the position, and I shall begin here and now to take pains over justice. I should like to think you will continue to do so; yet I am apprehensive, not from any distrust of your nature, but in view of the might of the state, lest it overcome both me and you.
